questions.question: What did research find about adding social skills training to exposure therapy for social anxiety?

options.text: It made no measurable difference to outcomes
It significantly reduced dropout rates (6:1) and improved actual social behavior as rated by independent observers
It only helped people with mild social anxiety
It was less effective than exposure therapy alone

questions.explanation: Research shows that adding social skills training to exposure therapy significantly reduces dropout rates by a ratio of 6:1 and improves actual social behavior as rated by independent observers. Skills and courage work together — practicing social skills alongside facing feared situations accelerates progress.

questions.question: Which of the three communication styles involves expressing your needs clearly while also respecting others?

options.text: Passive
Aggressive
Assertive
Avoidant

questions.explanation: Assertive communication involves expressing your needs, opinions, and boundaries clearly and respectfully — stating 'I'd prefer...' or 'I think...' while acknowledging the other person's perspective. This contrasts with passive (suppressing your needs to avoid conflict) and aggressive (expressing needs at the expense of others).

questions.question: What is the key insight about starting conversations, according to the lesson?

options.text: You need a brilliant, pre-rehearsed opening line to make a good impression
Starting a conversation does not have to be perfect — it just has to happen, and the other person will help carry it from there
You should always wait for the other person to start the conversation
Text messaging is always better than face-to-face conversation for people with social anxiety

questions.explanation: The key insight is that starting a conversation does not require a brilliant opening line. Simple, genuine approaches work — commenting on the shared environment, asking open-ended questions, or offering a genuine compliment. It just has to happen. The other person will help carry the conversation from there.

questions.question: How should you approach being the quieter person in a group conversation?

options.text: Force yourself to talk as much as the most talkative person
Leave the group and find a one-on-one conversation instead
Recognize that different conversation styles are normal — quality contributions, asking questions, and engaged listening are all valuable participation
Stay completely silent and wait for someone to directly ask you a question

questions.explanation: You do not need to match the most talkative person's output. Different conversation styles are normal. Quality over quantity — one thoughtful comment has more impact than constant chatter. Asking questions, being a good listener, and engaging physically (nodding, smiling, eye contact) all count as meaningful participation.

questions.question: When a social interaction genuinely does not go well, what is the healthiest way to respond?

options.text: 'The conversation was terrible because I'm socially incompetent'
'I should give up trying to socialize since I'm clearly bad at it'
Separate anxiety from skill — acknowledge 'The conversation didn't flow, which happens to everyone. I can practice specific skills.'
'I need to rehearse every conversation perfectly before attempting social interaction'

questions.explanation: The healthy response is to separate anxiety from skill. Rather than concluding 'I'm socially incompetent' (anxiety-driven belief), a more accurate assessment is 'The conversation didn't flow, which happens to everyone. I can practice specific skills.' If you notice a genuine area for growth, that is useful information — not evidence of failure.

title: Building Social Confidence Through Practice - Quiz
